paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1184 English Language — AI-authored Practice Paper 16: Three Fish from Tekka (Paper 2 §B style)

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about a Sec 3 girl who accompanies her father to Tekka wet market early on a Sunday morning to buy fish for her dying grandmother, and answer the questions that follow. Section B [19 marks] — AI-authored practice paper modelled on the Singapore-Cambridge 1184/02 (formerly 1128) Section B format and calibrated to the SEAB assessment objectives (literal / inferential comprehension, vocabulary in context, language for effect). Original passage written for the morning quiz bank; not from a past exam paper. Items mix mark values [1]/[2] as in the real paper: 10 short-answer questions (Q1-Q10) — 2 literal, 3 vocabulary-in-context, 3 inference and 2 language-for-effect — plus a 4-blank emotion-flowchart MCQ (Q11). Answer in your own words as far as possible.
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
At half past six on Sunday morning, Tekka was already loud. My father walked one step ahead of me, his canvas bag swinging at his side, and I followed him through the narrow aisles the way I had followed him since I was in Primary 1. The difference was that this morning we were buying fish for Ah Ma, who had not eaten in three days.

Paragraph 2
Ah Ma was in Ward 7B at the hospital. The doctors had stopped using the word 'recovery' a week ago. They used softer words now — comfort, manage, quality. My father and I had been sent to do what families in our part of Singapore have always done: buy something fresh, cook it gently, and bring it. As if a steamed fish could fix what the hospital could not.

Paragraph 3
We stopped at Auntie Goh's stall near the back. Auntie Goh had been selling fish to our family for fifteen years. She knew that Ah Ma had, for as long as I could remember, eaten only the white belly of a particular type of red snapper, which Auntie Goh kept under a separate sheet of ice for our family.

Paragraph 4
My father pointed at two snappers. 'These two, please,' he said. His voice was lower than usual. Auntie Goh nodded once, did not ask why we had not come last Sunday, did not ask after Ah Ma. She weighed the two fish and slid them into a clear plastic bag. Then, without a word, she reached under the second sheet of ice, lifted out a third smaller snapper, and dropped it into the bag.

Paragraph 5
My father opened his wallet. Auntie Goh shook her head and waved his hand away. 'For your Ah Ma,' she said. 'I know lah.' That was all. She turned to serve the next customer before my father could protest. I stood frozen with the styrofoam box at my feet, my cheeks suddenly hot, embarrassed that I did not know how to behave in the face of a kindness so plainly given.

Paragraph 6
We walked out into the morning light. The noodle uncle at the corner stall lifted his ladle in a small wave at my father — not the usual cheerful shout, just a small nod, as though he too had been told to be gentle. The prata auntie behind her hot griddle looked up, met my father's eyes for one long second, and then looked down again without smiling.

Paragraph 7
On the 65 bus home, I sat with the styrofoam box between my feet, the three fish sliding with every brake. Slowly I began to understand. The man who sold us banana leaves, the way he had given my father an extra leaf 'for luck'. The pharmacist auntie who had refused to charge for an extra bottle of saline. The kway teow uncle who had quietly added one extra prawn to our bowls every Saturday for a month. None of it had been coincidence. They had all known. They had been kind in small unspoken ways for weeks, and we had been too tired to notice.

Paragraph 8
When we reached our void deck in Tampines, my father set the box down. 'We will steam all three,' he said. 'Ah Ma always said three is a luckier number than two.' His voice was steady, but he did not look at me when he spoke. I picked up the box. The ice was half-melted, but the fish were still cold, and the morning, somehow, did not feel as heavy as it had at the market gates.

(AI-authored original narrative for O-Level 1184 §B style practice; not from a past examination paper. Tekka: a wet market in Little India; void deck: the open ground floor of an HDB block; prata: a South Indian griddle bread popular in Singapore.)

questions.stem: Q1. From Paragraph 2, in which hospital ward was Ah Ma? [1]
Q2. From Paragraph 3, which part of the red snapper had Ah Ma always eaten? [1]
Q3. What does the word 'frozen' (Paragraph 5) suggest about the narrator? [1]
questions.answer: unable to move / motionless with surprise

questions.stem: Q4. What does the phrase 'softer words' (Paragraph 2) mean? [1]
Q5. What does the phrase 'waved his hand away' (Paragraph 5) suggest Auntie Goh did? [1]
Q6. Using your own words, explain why the doctors now use 'softer words' such as comfort, manage and quality (Paragraph 2). [2]
questions.answer: ① they no longer expect Ah Ma to recover; ② so they avoid the harsh truth and gently prepare the family for her death

questions.stem: Q7. Using your own words, explain why the narrator's cheeks became 'suddenly hot' after Auntie Goh refused payment (Paragraph 5). [2]
questions.answer: ① she was caught off guard by such open kindness; ② she felt awkward, not knowing how to respond or behave

questions.stem: Q8. Using your own words, explain what the narrator finally came to understand on the bus home (Paragraph 7). [2]
questions.answer: ① many neighbours had quietly known Ah Ma was dying for weeks; ② they had been showing small kindnesses the tired family had failed to notice

questions.stem: Q9. What is the effect of the sentence 'As if a steamed fish could fix what the hospital could not' (Paragraph 2)? [2]
questions.answer: the irony underlines the family's helplessness — a fish cannot cure Ah Ma, yet it is the only loving thing they can still do, exposing the gap between medicine and tradition

questions.stem: Q10. What is the effect of ending the passage with 'the morning, somehow, did not feel as heavy as it had at the market gates' (Paragraph 8)? [2]
questions.answer: though nothing has changed, the contrast shows the neighbours' quiet kindness has eased the family's emotional burden, leaving the narrator feeling quietly supported

sections.instruction: Q11. The narrator's dominant feeling shifts as the Sunday morning unfolds. For each part of the passage, choose ONE word from the bank that best summarises her dominant feeling at that moment. There are some extra words in the bank you do not need to use. Word bank: dutiful / bored / embarrassed / suspicious / moved / annoyed / grateful / triumphant. The original task carries 4 marks; each blank is split into its own MCQ here so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same 'Three Fish from Tekka' narrative in Exercise 1 above. The narrator's feelings shift over the course of the morning — from a quiet sense of duty as she follows her father into the market for fish for Ah Ma, to embarrassment when Auntie Goh refuses payment and adds a third fish, to being deeply moved on the bus home as she realises how many neighbours had been showing quiet kindness for weeks, ending in a quiet gratitude back at the void deck. Pick the single best word from the bank for each section.

questions.stem: Q11(i). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 1-2, as she follows her father into Tekka at half past six on Sunday morning to buy fish for Ah Ma? [1]

Q11(ii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 5, as she stands frozen with the styrofoam box at her feet after Auntie Goh waves her father's payment away and adds a third snapper to the bag? [1]

Q11(iii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 7, on the 65 bus home as she realises that the banana-leaf seller, the pharmacist auntie, and the kway teow uncle had all been quietly kind to the family for weeks? [1]

Q11(iv). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling at the end of Paragraph 8, back at the void deck in Tampines as she lifts the styrofoam box and notices that the morning no longer feels as heavy as it did at the market gates? [1]
